summary: Gruber dissects Adobe CEO Shantanu Narayen's claims about Flash progress on iPhone, arguing that technical achievement in the simulator is meaningless without Apple's permission. He systematically examines every possible path Adobe could take — browser plugin, standalone player, or full browser — and demonstrates that each one is blocked by Apple's SDK restrictions and deliberate architectural choices. The piece highlights that MobileSafari doesn't support any plugins, that Apple doesn't even allow inline QuickTime playback, and that the SDK explicitly prohibits interpreted code execution. Gruber is baffled that Narayen would publicly discuss technical progress when the real bottleneck is entirely political — Apple simply doesn't want Flash on the iPhone.
key_points: Getting Flash running in the iPhone simulator has no meaningful technical significance since the simulator runs on desktop hardware
MobileSafari supports no plugins of any kind, and the iPhone SDK provides no way for third parties to modify it
Apple doesn't even allow inline QuickTime playback in MobileSafari, making Flash support even less likely
The iPhone SDK Agreement explicitly prohibits apps from executing interpreted code, blocking both standalone Flash players and Flash-enabled browsers
Every possible path to Flash on iPhone requires Apple's explicit permission, which Apple shows no interest in granting
money_quotes: Talking about technical progress only serves to focus attention on the fact that it is Apple's decision, and by all appearances, Apple does not want Flash on the iPhone.

Even if Adobe eventually gets Flash running well — by any standard for "running well" — on actual iPhone hardware, rather than just in the iPhone simulator, they can't ship it without Apple's explicit permission.
The iPhone is simply too performance sensitive to allow for inline media playback.
The more I think about it, the more baffled I am that Narayen said anything specific at all.
There is no way for third-party developers to modify MobileSafari or the content it is capable of displaying via the iPhone SDK.
key_insight: Adobe's public claims about Flash progress on iPhone are strategically foolish because every possible implementation path is blocked not by technical limitations but by Apple's deliberate policy choices.
